captured.evidence: enters the central compartment first — the blood and vessel-rich group including brain, heart, kidneys, and liver. This group receives about 75% of cardiac output despite being only 10% of body mass

wakes up in 8 minutes NOT because of metabolism — hepatic metabolism is too slow. It is redistribution: propofol moves from the highly perfused brain into less-perfused peripheral tissues like muscle and fat, dropping brain concentration below the threshold for consciousness
context-sensitive half-time — the time for plasma concentration to fall 50% after stopping an infusion of a given duration
after 4 hours the peripheral compartments have progressively saturated with propofol. The redistribution gradient is gone. Recovery depends on slower hepatic metabolism
First-pass pulmonary uptake buffers peak arterial concentrations of lipophilic drugs — fentanyl undergoes 65-75% first-pass pulmonary uptake protecting the brain
elderly patients have smaller effective central compartments, producing higher peak concentrations from the same dose — dose reduction is essential

summary: Excellent comprehensive answer covering all key pharmacokinetic principles with accurate details. You correctly emphasized redistribution vs metabolism and explained context-sensitive half-time perfectly.
voss_quip: Finally, someone who understands redistribution isn't just a fancy word.
